extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box that houses a heat‑bed for heating and a fan to circulate air. Temperature is measured by two sensors inside the box and one sensor outside. The plant is controlled by a Raspberry Pi running a bang‑bang‑style controller that receives temperature measurements and drives the heat‑bed and fan via a low‑level driver. The goal of the PT is to reach and maintain a target temperature inside the box irrespective of the contents, making it a representative cyber‑physical system for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin (DT) provides real‑time visualization of incubator status via a time‑series database and dashboard, on‑demand calibration of the temperature models, anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter, predictive what‑if simulations through co‑simulation of historical or live PT data, and a self‑adaptation loop that detects behavioral changes, schedules experiments, recalibrates models, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and optimizes controller parameters. These services support monitoring, prediction, optimisation, control and visual analytics for operators and developers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation relies on the RabbitMQ message broker as the communication server, a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator I/O and publishes data to RabbitMQ, and a Raspberry Pi‑based controller that subscribes to commands and state updates. Calibration algorithms update Model A and Model B parameters; Model B is refined with a Kalman filter for heat‑bed temperature estimation. Real‑time data are stored in a time‑series database and displayed on a web‑based dashboard. What‑if simulations are executed via co‑simulation components that ingest PT data. All hardware designs, software, and datasets are open‑source and hosted in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The incubator Digital Twin is orchestrated around a RabbitMQ message broker that synchronises the physical twin (PT) components—the low‑level driver that reads sensor data and issues actuator commands—and the cyber components such as the controller, calibration service, Kalman‑filter based anomaly detector, co‑simulation engine, and visualization dashboard. All cyber services subscribe to the same RabbitMQ topics, allowing a loosely‑coupled, publish‑subscribe architecture where data flows from the PT to a time‑series database for real‑time visualisation and to analysis services. The DT components operate as independent processes that consume the same streams of measurement and command messages, providing a centralized coordination point (the broker) without a single monolithic application. This constitutes the system‑wide coordination and resource management pattern described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin integrates two analytical behavioral models of the incubator temperature dynamics (Model A and Model B) derived from heat‑energy equations; Model B includes the heat‑bed thermal inertia and is preferred for higher fidelity. Sensor data (temperature readings from two internal and one external sensors) are streamed by the low‑level driver to a RabbitMQ message broker and persisted in a time‑series database for real‑time visualization dashboards. Calibration algorithms automatically adjust model parameters on‑demand (section 5) and the calibrated Model B feeds a Kalman filter that performs heat‑bed state estimation and anomaly detection. Co‑simulation (what‑if) runs combine historical or live PT data with the models to optimise controller parameters. Thus the DT constellation comprises data acquisition, storage, calibrated analytical models, and algorithmic components (calibration, Kalman filtering, optimisation) that are tightly coupled.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a heatbed (electrical heating element) and a fan that distributes warm air inside the insulated styrofoam box. Control logic resides in a Raspberry Pi‑based bang‑bang controller with configurable parameters: lower temperature limit (LL), upper limit (UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). Actuation commands are sent via RabbitMQ from the controller to the low‑level driver, which issues the commands to the heatbed and fan. The low‑level driver also enforces a safety protocol to keep the device in a safe state. Remote actuation is achieved through the communication server (RabbitMQ) that mediates messages between controller and driver.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is provided by three sensors: two placed inside the incubator box and one outside to monitor ambient room temperature. Sensors measure temperature in °C (or K) and their readings are periodically read by the low‑level driver, which uploads the data to the RabbitMQ server for downstream processing. Specific sampling rates, transmission bandwidth, and sensor accuracy specifications are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by comparing Model A (simpler) and Model B (includes heat‑bed inertia); experimental results (section 4) show Model B yields higher prediction accuracy for thermal dynamics. Model validity is ensured through on‑demand calibration that updates model parameters using observed sensor data, and through Kalman‑filter‑based state estimation that detects anomalies. Uncertainties stem from modeling assumptions (uniform temperature distribution, negligible wall heat storage, constant specific heat capacity, and power‑to‑heat conversion). Validation is performed via empirical experiments and calibration results (Figure 13). Errors are identified by the anomaly detection module, and model updates are performed to maintain accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data measured by two internal sensors and one external sensor are sampled by the low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi at a periodic rate and published as messages to the RabbitMQ message broker. The messages contain raw temperature values (in °C) and are transmitted using RabbitMQ’s native messaging protocol (AMQP over TCP). DT components subscribe to these topics, receive the sensor readings, optionally compute the average of the two internal sensors to obtain the box air temperature, and feed the resulting data into calibration, monitoring and simulation algorithms. The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol to ensure that only valid sensor states are sent.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DT issues actuation commands (e.g., turn heat‑bed on/off, set heating duration, activate fan) by publishing command messages to RabbitMQ. The controller on the Raspberry Pi subscribes to these command topics, validates the payload against expected formats (temperature limits, heating/waiting durations), and forwards the resulting control signals to the hardware drivers that drive the heat‑bed and fan. After execution the controller publishes status updates back to RabbitMQ, allowing the DT to confirm command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components of the incubator DT are interconnected through a RabbitMQ message broker deployed on‑premise (or in the cloud). Communication uses RabbitMQ’s AMQP protocol over TCP/IP, forming a star‑like topology where the Raspberry Pi (low‑level driver and controller) and DT services are clients that publish/subscribe to the broker. RabbitMQ provides message persistence and acknowledgement, offering reliable delivery with low latency suitable for near‑real‑time control. Security is handled by RabbitMQ’s built‑in authentication (username/password), though specific mechanisms are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT engineering process for the incubator case study follows a model‑centric workflow: first, two mathematical models of the temperature dynamics (Model A and Model B) are built based on heat‑transfer equations and a set of simplifying assumptions (uniform air temperature, negligible wall heat storage, constant air specific heat, and either full or partial power‑to‑heat conversion). Calibration algorithms are then applied to update model parameters using sensor data collected by the low‑level driver. The calibrated models feed monitors that assess safety and optimality, while the controller (a bang‑bang‑style regulator with configurable lower/upper temperature limits, heating duration H and waiting duration C) generates actuation commands. All runtime data and control messages are exchanged through a RabbitMQ message broker, allowing additional DT components to subscribe to the same streams. Calibration can be triggered on‑demand, and co‑simulation runs can replay historical or live PT data for what‑if analysis. The overall process is iterative: data acquisition → model calibration → monitoring → decision (human or algorithmic) → possible re‑configuration of the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator DT provides several decision‑support capabilities: (1) real‑time visualisation of temperature readings and controller states on a dashboard by streaming time‑series data from the low‑level driver to a database; (2) on‑demand calibration of Model B using the latest sensor measurements; (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation via a Kalman filter that fuses Model B predictions with measured temperatures; (4) what‑if simulations performed through co‑simulation that combine historical or live PT data with the calibrated models to evaluate alternative controller parameters; and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that, upon detecting a behavioural change (e.g., introduction of a cold object), automatically schedules an experiment, re‑calibrates the model, updates the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations and finally re‑configures the controller. These mechanisms together generate actionable insights and drive automatic or human‑in‑the‑loop decisions.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin implementation for the incubator incorporates safety measures through a 'safe protocol' embedded in the low‑level driver, which continuously monitors temperature readings and command messages to ensure the physical twin remains in a safe state before issuing actuator commands. Communication between the Raspberry Pi controller and the physical twin is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker, providing reliable message passing; however, the documents do not detail authentication, encryption, or access‑control mechanisms, so only the presence of the safe protocol and the use of RabbitMQ are documented safety‑related aspects.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
